mechanism: Potent induction of CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein (P-gp/ABCB1) by hyperforin component; accelerated first-pass and systemic metabolism of cyclosporine, dramatically reducing plasma AUC by 50–70%
effect: Subtherapeutic cyclosporine levels leading to acute transplant rejection; multiple documented cases of solid-organ rejection within days of SJW initiation
management: Absolute contraindication in transplant patients. Discontinue SJW immediately. Monitor cyclosporine trough levels daily until stable. Dose adjustment rarely sufficient — avoid combination entirely.
references: Piscitelli SC et al. Lancet 2000;355:547–548
Ruschitzka F et al. Lancet 2000;355:548–549

mechanism: Hyperforin induces CYP2C9 (primary warfarin S-enantiomer metabolism) and CYP3A4, increasing warfarin clearance. Also induces P-gp, reducing intestinal absorption. Net effect: 30–50% reduction in warfarin AUC.
effect: Significantly reduced anticoagulation effect; INR falls to subtherapeutic range within 2–3 weeks, increasing thromboembolic risk (stroke, DVT, PE). Conversely, abrupt discontinuation of SJW can cause INR to surge and precipitate bleeding.
management: Avoid combination. If SJW must be stopped in a patient already on both, reduce warfarin dose proactively and monitor INR every 2–3 days for 2–4 weeks. Educate patient on risks.
references: Yue QY et al. Lancet 2000;355:576–577
Jiang X et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2004;75:546–557

mechanism: Additive serotonergic activity: SJW inhibits neuronal reuptake of serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine (via hypericin and hyperforin). Combined with SSRI-mediated serotonin reuptake inhibition, produces serotonin syndrome through excessive synaptic 5-HT accumulation.
effect: Serotonin syndrome: triad of neuromuscular abnormalities (clonus, hyperreflexia, myoclonus), autonomic instability (hyperthermia, tachycardia, diaphoresis), and altered mental status. Potentially life-threatening if severe.
management: Contraindicated. If patient is on an SSRI, SJW must not be added. Transition protocol requires at least 2-week washout (5 weeks for fluoxetine) before switching. Emergency management of serotonin syndrome: cyproheptadine, supportive care.
references: Izzo AA et al. Br J Pharmacol 2004;144:647–650
Sternbach H. Am J Psychiatry 1991;148:705–713

mechanism: Hyperforin induces CYP3A4 (primary metabolic pathway for most antiretrovirals) and P-gp; reduces intestinal absorption and increases systemic clearance. Indinavir AUC reduced by 57% in controlled studies.
effect: Subtherapeutic antiretroviral plasma levels leading to virologic failure and selection of resistant HIV strains. Risk of treatment failure and disease progression.
management: Absolute contraindication in HIV-positive patients on antiretroviral therapy. FDA issued formal contraindication advisory. Patients must be counselled at initiation of ART — screen for all herbal use.
references: Piscitelli SC et al. Lancet 2000;355:547–548
de Maat MM et al. AIDS 2001;15:420–421

mechanism: Induction of CYP3A4 accelerates metabolism of both estrogen and progestin components; P-gp induction reduces intestinal absorption. Ethinyl estradiol AUC reduced by approximately 13–50%; progestin components affected similarly.
effect: Contraceptive failure; multiple documented cases of unintended pregnancy in women using SJW concurrently. Also causes breakthrough bleeding as an early warning sign.
management: Warn all female patients of reproductive age. Switch to non-hormonal contraception while using SJW or discontinue SJW. UK MHRA and WHO list this as a contraindication requiring patient counselling.
references: Schwarz UI et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2003;74:525–535
Murphy PA et al. Contraception 2005;71:402–408

mechanism: P-glycoprotein induction by hyperforin reduces intestinal digoxin absorption and increases renal tubular secretion. Digoxin is a prototypical P-gp substrate; AUC reduced by 25–33% in 10-day crossover studies.
effect: Subtherapeutic digoxin levels, inadequate rate control in atrial fibrillation, or loss of inotropic effect in heart failure. Risk is highest when SJW is abruptly discontinued — rebound increase in digoxin levels may cause toxicity.
management: Avoid combination. Monitor digoxin trough levels frequently. If SJW is stopped, reduce digoxin dose by approximately 25% and recheck levels within 1 week.
references: Johne A et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 1999;66:338–345
Mueller SC et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2004;75:546

mechanism: Hyperforin induces CYP3A4 (converts irinotecan to inactive metabolites) and UGT1A9 (glucuronidates active SN-38 metabolite), dramatically reducing active drug exposure. Irinotecan AUC reduced by 40% in oncology patients.
effect: Significantly reduced chemotherapeutic efficacy with risk of treatment failure in cancer patients. SN-38 (active toxic metabolite) AUC reduced by 42%, potentially allowing tumour progression.
management: Absolute contraindication in cancer patients on chemotherapy. National Cancer Institute and oncology guidelines explicitly prohibit concurrent use. Screen all cancer patients for herbal use before initiating chemotherapy.
references: Mathijssen RH et al. J Natl Cancer Inst 2002;94:1247–1249
Frye RF et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2004;76:428–440

mechanism: CYP3A4 induction by hyperforin increases metabolism of triazolobenzodiazepines (alprazolam, midazolam, triazolam are CYP3A4 substrates). Alprazolam AUC reduced by approximately 50% after 14 days of SJW coadministration.
effect: Reduced anxiolytic and sedative efficacy; patients may experience return of anxiety symptoms, insomnia, or withdrawal-like effects despite taking prescribed doses.
management: Monitor for reduced benzodiazepine effect. If combination unavoidable, dose increase of benzodiazepine may be necessary under medical supervision. Prefer benzodiazepines metabolised by glucuronidation (oxazepam, lorazepam, temazepam) which are not affected.
references: Markowitz JS et al. JAMA 2003;290:1500–1504
Wang Z et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2001;70:317–326

mechanism: Methadone is a substrate of CYP3A4, CYP2C9, and CYP2D6; SJW induces CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, markedly accelerating methadone clearance. Also reduces P-gp-mediated CNS distribution.
effect: Opioid withdrawal syndrome in methadone maintenance patients; inadequate pain control. Multiple case reports of acute opioid withdrawal precipitated within 1–3 weeks of starting SJW.
management: Contraindicated in patients on methadone maintenance. Screen all opioid-dependent patients. If SJW discovered, gradual dose increase of methadone under addiction medicine supervision may be needed. Do not abruptly stop SJW.
references: Eich-Höchli D et al. Pharmacopsychiatry 2003;36:35–37
Izzo AA. Phytomedicine 2004;11:98–120

mechanism: Dual serotonergic mechanism: SJW inhibits SERT and NET (same targets as SNRIs), producing additive serotonin accumulation. Additionally, CYP2D6 inhibition by some SJW constituents may reduce venlafaxine-to-ODV conversion, further altering serotonergic tone.
effect: Serotonin syndrome risk equivalent to SSRI combination. Case reports include agitation, tremor, hyperthermia, and hypertensive crisis.
management: Contraindicated. Educate patients that SJW is not a safe complement to prescribed antidepressants. Require 2-week washout before switching between agents.
references: Lantz MS et al. J Geriatr Psychiatry Neurol 1999;12:7–10
Barbenel DM et al. J Psychopharmacol 2000;14:84–86

mechanism: Triptans are partial serotonin agonists at 5-HT1B/1D receptors; SJW's serotonergic reuptake inhibition increases synaptic serotonin. Combined effect may produce weak serotonin syndrome or cardiovascular serotonergic effects including coronary vasospasm.
effect: Increased risk of mild-to-moderate serotonin toxicity symptoms: flushing, tachycardia, hypertension, weakness. Also potential for reduced triptan efficacy if SJW-mediated CYP3A4 induction reduces triptan plasma levels.
management: Avoid concurrent use. If patient uses both for migraine and depression/anxiety, discuss with prescribing physician. Advise patient not to take SJW on migraine days when triptan is used.
references: Gardner DM et al. CMAJ 2000;163:1199
Izzo AA et al. Br J Pharmacol 2004;144:647–650

mechanism: Ginkgolides (particularly ginkgolide B) are potent platelet-activating factor (PAF) antagonists, inhibiting PAF-mediated platelet aggregation. Flavonoid fraction inhibits thromboxane synthesis. Combined with warfarin's anticoagulant effect produces pharmacodynamic synergy. Some evidence of CYP2C9 modulation by ginkgo flavonoids.
effect: Significantly increased bleeding risk: spontaneous hyphema, subdural haematoma, subarachnoid haemorrhage, and surgical bleeding complications reported. INR may not reflect true bleeding risk because platelet function is impaired independently of coagulation cascade.
management: Avoid combination. If patient insists, monitor INR and assess platelet function. Discontinue ginkgo at least 36 hours before any surgery or invasive procedure. Screen all anticoagulated patients for ginkgo use.
references: Rosenblatt M et al. N Engl J Med 1997;336:1108
Jiang X et al. J Clin Pharmacol 2005;45:1484–1491

mechanism: Pharmacodynamic synergy: aspirin irreversibly acetylates COX-1 impairing thromboxane A2-mediated platelet aggregation; ginkgolide B antagonises PAF-receptor on platelets. Two independent antiplatelet mechanisms acting simultaneously, producing additive inhibition of platelet function beyond either agent alone.
effect: Increased spontaneous bleeding, petechiae, purpura, prolonged bleeding from minor cuts, epistaxis. Case reports of spontaneous subdural haematoma in patients on aspirin plus ginkgo.
management: Caution, especially in patients on high-dose aspirin (>100 mg/day). Monitor for bleeding signs. Discontinue ginkgo 7 days before elective surgery. Advise patient to avoid ginkgo if on dual antiplatelet therapy.
references: Kleijnen J et al. BMJ 1992;340:1136
Matthews MK. Neurology 1998;50:1933–1934

mechanism: Triple mechanism of additive antiplatelet effect: ginkgolide B blocks PAF receptor, flavonoids inhibit thromboxane synthesis, and clopidogrel irreversibly blocks platelet P2Y12 receptor. Ginkgo may also inhibit CYP2C19 which is required for clopidogrel bioactivation — paradoxically this could reduce efficacy while simultaneously increasing bleeding risk through independent PAF antagonism.
effect: Substantially increased bleeding risk; particularly dangerous in post-ACS or post-stent patients who are often on dual antiplatelet therapy. Subdural haematoma, GI bleeding, and surgical haemorrhage reported.
management: Avoid in patients on any antiplatelet therapy. Highest risk with dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel). Mandatory cessation 7–10 days pre-operatively.
references: Kim YS et al. Phytomedicine 2012;19:1140–1145
Jiang X et al. J Clin Pharmacol 2005;45:1484–1491

mechanism: NSAIDs reversibly inhibit COX-1 impairing platelet thromboxane A2 synthesis; ginkgolides block PAF-receptor. Additive antiplatelet pharmacodynamics. NSAIDs also impair gastric mucosal integrity, increasing GI bleeding risk that is compounded by antiplatelet effect of ginkgo.
effect: Increased GI bleeding risk; potential for prolonged surgical bleeding. Less critical than warfarin combination but clinically significant, particularly in elderly patients.
management: Monitor for GI bleeding symptoms (melaena, haematemesis). Consider gastroprotection (PPI) if combination unavoidable. Advise patients to report unusual bruising or prolonged bleeding.
references: Kupiec T et al. Forensic Sci Int 2005;151:39–42
Izzo AA. Phytomedicine 2004;11:98–120

mechanism: SSRIs deplete platelet serotonin stores by blocking SERT on platelets, impairing platelet activation (serotonin amplifies ADP-induced aggregation). Ginkgo independently inhibits PAF-mediated aggregation. Dual impairment of platelet function through mechanistically distinct pathways.
effect: Increased risk of abnormal uterine bleeding, GI bleeding, surgical haemorrhage. SSRIs alone double GI bleed risk; combination with ginkgo further amplifies this, especially with concurrent NSAID use.
management: Monitor for signs of bleeding, particularly GI symptoms. Consider gastroprotection in high-risk patients. Avoid triple combination of SSRI + ginkgo + NSAID.
references: Helin-Salmivaara A et al. Pharmacoepidemiol Drug Saf 2007;16:347–354
Mackenzie J. Am J Geriatr Psychiatry 2002;10:237–238

mechanism: Two mechanisms: (1) Ginkgo seeds contain 4'-O-methylpyridoxine (ginkgotoxin), a neurotoxin that antagonises GABA synthesis by inhibiting pyridoxal phosphate-dependent enzymes, lowering seizure threshold. (2) Ginkgo may induce CYP2C19 and CYP3A4, reducing carbamazepine and phenytoin plasma levels.
effect: Breakthrough seizures reported even in well-controlled epileptic patients. Case reports of convulsions in patients starting ginkgo supplements contaminated with ginkgotoxin. Drug level subtherapeutic range due to enzyme induction.
management: Avoid in patients with epilepsy or on antiepileptic drugs. If ginkgo used, monitor antiepileptic drug levels and seizure frequency. Ensure supplement is from standardised extract (EGb 761) with minimal ginkgotoxin.
references: Granger AS. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry 2001;70:680–681
Kupiec T et al. Forensic Sci Int 2005;151:39–42

mechanism: Ginkgo flavonoids and terpenoids modulate insulin signalling, improving peripheral glucose uptake via GLUT4 translocation. May enhance insulin secretion and sensitise peripheral tissues. Additive glucose-lowering effects with antidiabetic medications.
effect: Hypoglycaemia risk, particularly with insulin and sulfonylureas. Symptoms: diaphoresis, tremor, confusion, palpitations. Risk compounded by exercise or fasting.
management: Monitor blood glucose more frequently when ginkgo is introduced or discontinued. Patient education on hypoglycaemia recognition and management. Dose adjustment of antidiabetic drug may be necessary.
references: Kudolo GB et al. Clin Nutr 2005;24:943–952
Kudolo GB et al. J Clin Pharmacol 2002;42:647–654

mechanism: Ginkgo flavonoids modestly inhibit CYP2C19, the primary enzyme responsible for omeprazole and other PPIs metabolism. In CYP2C19 intermediate or poor metabolisers, the effect is amplified.
effect: Increased omeprazole plasma levels (up to 2-fold in some studies), potentially increasing efficacy but also adverse effects (headache, diarrhoea, hypomagnesaemia with long-term use).
management: Generally clinically insignificant at standard ginkgo doses. Monitor for PPI-related adverse effects if patient takes high-dose ginkgo. Genotyping for CYP2C19 may be relevant in specialised settings.
references: Yin OQ et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2004;76:654–663

mechanism: Allicin, ajoene, and diallyl disulfide in garlic inhibit platelet aggregation by suppressing TXA2 synthesis and antagonising fibrinogen receptor (GPIIb/IIIa). Additionally, some evidence of CYP2C9 inhibition and reduction in platelet count. Pharmacodynamic synergy with warfarin's anticoagulant action.
effect: Markedly increased bleeding risk; INR elevation has been documented. Postoperative haemorrhage, spontaneous haematoma, and GI bleeding reported. Risk amplified with therapeutic-dose warfarin.
management: Avoid high-dose garlic supplements in anticoagulated patients. Dietary garlic at normal culinary doses presents minimal risk. Discontinue supplements 7–10 days before surgery. Monitor INR if combination unavoidable.
references: Burnham BE. J Am Acad Dermatol 1995;33:683
Ackermann RT et al. Arch Intern Med 2001;161:813–824

mechanism: Garlic constituents (particularly allyl sulphides) induce CYP3A4 and intestinal P-glycoprotein, reducing saquinavir bioavailability by approximately 51% (Piscitelli et al. study). Saquinavir has inherently poor and variable bioavailability that is critically sensitive to CYP3A4 activity.
effect: Plasma saquinavir AUC reduced by 51%, trough levels by 49%. Risk of HIV virologic failure and development of protease inhibitor resistance. Rebound effect when garlic stopped may temporarily increase drug levels.
management: Contraindicated in HIV patients on protease inhibitor-based regimens. FDA advisory warns against garlic supplements during ART. Dietary garlic in small amounts may be acceptable but advise caution.
references: Piscitelli SC et al. Clin Infect Dis 2002;34:234–238

mechanism: Ajoene and diallyl trisulfide are structurally analogous to prostacyclin and competitively inhibit TXA2-mediated platelet activation. Additive with antiplatelet drugs acting via distinct mechanisms (COX-1 inhibition by aspirin, P2Y12 blockade by clopidogrel).
effect: Amplified platelet inhibition leading to increased bleeding tendency. Risk highest with dual antiplatelet therapy. Perioperative haemorrhage risk in patients on aspirin plus garlic supplements.
management: Discontinue garlic supplements 7 days before surgery. Monitor for bruising and petechiae. Educate patients to disclose supplement use to all healthcare providers.
references: Bordia A et al. Prostaglandins Leukot Essent Fatty Acids 1998;58:257–263

mechanism: Allicin enhances insulin secretion from pancreatic beta-cells and improves peripheral insulin sensitivity via PPAR-γ activation and AMPK pathway modulation. Additive glucose-lowering effect with antidiabetic medications.
effect: Enhanced hypoglycaemia risk, particularly with insulin and sulfonylureas. Studies show garlic reduces fasting blood glucose by 10–15% in diabetic patients.
management: Increase frequency of blood glucose monitoring when garlic supplementation is introduced. May allow dose reduction of antidiabetic drug — adjust under medical supervision. Beneficial interaction but requires monitoring.
references: Ashraf R et al. Pak J Pharm Sci 2011;24:565–570
Orekhov AN et al. Drugs Exp Clin Res 1995;21:257–262

mechanism: Allicin inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) activity, relaxes vascular smooth muscle via H2S release (NO-independent vasodilation), and reduces peripheral vascular resistance. Additive hypotensive effects with antihypertensive medications.
effect: Orthostatic hypotension, dizziness, syncope — particularly on initial dosing. Blood pressure may fall below target range.
management: Monitor blood pressure when garlic supplementation is introduced or stopped. Educate patient on position changes and hypotension symptoms. Usually manageable without discontinuation.
references: Ried K et al. J Nutr 2010;140:821S–826S
Warshafsky S et al. Ann Intern Med 1993;119:599–605

mechanism: Garlic constituents modulate CYP2E1 activity, which is involved in isoniazid-mediated hepatotoxicity (via reactive metabolites). Garlic also has independent hepatoprotective effects via antioxidant mechanisms that may attenuate isoniazid hepatotoxicity. Pharmacokinetic interaction with rifampicin via CYP3A4 modulation is less characterised.
effect: Potential reduction in isoniazid-induced hepatotoxicity (possibly beneficial). However, if CYP2E1 is induced, reactive metabolite formation may increase. Net clinical effect uncertain.
management: Monitor liver function tests in patients on anti-TB therapy. Garlic at culinary doses unlikely to be problematic; high-dose supplementation warrants caution.
references: Kasuga S et al. Phytomedicine 2001;8:297–305

mechanism: Gingerols and shogaols inhibit platelet aggregation by suppressing thromboxane A2 synthesis via COX-1 inhibition and reducing platelet TXA2/PGI2 ratio. Pharmacodynamic interaction with warfarin's coagulation cascade inhibition. Some evidence of weak CYP2C9 inhibition at high doses.
effect: Increased bleeding tendency; INR elevation reported in patients taking ginger supplements concurrently with warfarin. Dietary ginger at culinary doses unlikely to be problematic.
management: Advise patients on warfarin to avoid high-dose ginger supplements (>4 g/day dried ginger equivalent). Culinary use (cooking amounts) is generally safe. Monitor INR if patient uses ginger tea or supplements regularly.
references: Vaes LP et al. Ann Pharmacother 2000;34:1478–1482
Heck AM et al. Am J Health Syst Pharm 2000;57:1221–1227

mechanism: Gingerols inhibit platelet TXA2 synthesis via COX-1 inhibition, mechanistically similar to aspirin but reversible. Additive antiplatelet pharmacodynamics when combined with aspirin (irreversible COX-1 inhibitor) or clopidogrel (P2Y12 blocker).
effect: Prolonged bleeding time, increased bruising, petechiae. Risk particularly relevant in patients on dual antiplatelet therapy post-ACS or post-stent insertion.
management: Avoid high-dose ginger supplements with antiplatelet therapy. Discontinue at least 7 days before surgery. Normal dietary ginger consumption is likely safe.
references: Bordia A et al. Prostaglandins Leukot Essent Fatty Acids 1997;56:379–384

mechanism: Gingerol compounds enhance GLUT4 translocation to cell membrane and activate AMPK pathway, improving insulin sensitivity and peripheral glucose uptake. Mild additive hypoglycaemic effect with antidiabetic medications.
effect: Mild hypoglycaemia risk with sulfonylureas or insulin. Clinical studies show ginger reduces fasting blood glucose by 10–12% in type 2 diabetes. Interaction risk is usually manageable.
management: Monitor blood glucose more closely when ginger supplementation is initiated. Possibly beneficial in type 2 diabetes but dose adjustment of antidiabetic drug may occasionally be needed.
references: Mahluji S et al. Int J Food Sci Nutr 2013;64:682–686
Akhani SP et al. J Pharm Pharmacol 2004;56:101–105

mechanism: Gingerols have demonstrated calcium channel blocking activity in animal studies; 6-shogaol inhibits voltage-dependent L-type Ca²⁺ channels in vascular smooth muscle. Possible additive vasodilatory effect with CCBs. Also modest CYP3A4 inhibition may reduce CCB clearance.
effect: Enhanced antihypertensive effect, dizziness, peripheral oedema (classic CCB adverse effects). Risk of orthostatic hypotension, particularly in elderly.
management: Monitor blood pressure when high-dose ginger is introduced. Educate patient on position changes. Usually clinically insignificant at culinary doses.
references: Ghayur MN et al. Vascul Pharmacol 2005;43:234–241

mechanism: Gingerols and shogaols act as 5-HT3 receptor antagonists (same mechanism as ondansetron) and also modulate gastric motility via muscarinic and histamine H1 pathways. When combined with 5-HT3 antagonist antiemetics, additive antiemetic effect through complementary receptor-level activity.
effect: Generally beneficial — enhanced control of chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV). Risk of constipation and delayed gastric emptying with high doses of both agents.
management: Potentially useful adjunct for CINV management. Monitor for constipation. No dose adjustment typically needed but document concurrent use in oncology records.
references: Ryan JL et al. Support Care Cancer 2012;20:1479–1489
Zick SM et al. Cancer 2009;115:3760–3769

mechanism: Curcumin inhibits platelet aggregation by suppressing TXA2 and reducing platelet surface expression of P-selectin. Additionally inhibits CYP2C9 (primary enzyme for warfarin S-enantiomer hydroxylation) and CYP3A4, potentially raising warfarin plasma levels. Pharmacokinetic + pharmacodynamic dual interaction.
effect: INR elevation, increased bleeding risk. Case reports of clinically significant INR elevation in patients adding curcumin supplements to warfarin therapy. Risk amplified with bioavailability-enhanced formulations (piperine-enhanced, liposomal, BCM-95).
management: Monitor INR closely when curcumin supplementation is started or stopped. If using bioavailability-enhanced curcumin, treat with same caution as warfarin-NSAID combination. Culinary turmeric poses minimal risk.
references: Zhang Y et al. Drug Metab Dispos 2007;35:1049–1058
Srinivasan M et al. J Herb Pharmacother 2005;5:1–5

mechanism: Curcumin inhibits intestinal P-glycoprotein (P-gp/ABCB1) and CYP3A4 in the gut wall, increasing tacrolimus oral bioavailability. Tacrolimus has a narrow therapeutic index and is critically dependent on P-gp and CYP3A4 for first-pass limitation of absorption.
effect: Elevated tacrolimus trough levels leading to tacrolimus toxicity: nephrotoxicity (rising serum creatinine), neurotoxicity (tremor, headache), hypertension, hyperglycaemia.
management: Avoid high-dose curcumin in transplant patients. If used medicinally, monitor tacrolimus trough levels weekly until stable. Dose reduction of tacrolimus may be required.
references: Egashira K et al. Drug Metab Pharmacokinet 2012;27:440–447

mechanism: Curcumin inhibits both COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes, NF-κB pathway, and arachidonic acid cascade — mechanisms shared with NSAIDs. Additive anti-inflammatory pharmacodynamics. COX-1 inhibition potentiates NSAID-induced gastric mucosal injury. Also additive platelet inhibition via TXA2 suppression.
effect: Paradoxical: curcumin may be gastroprotective at low doses but at high supplemental doses combined with NSAIDs, risk of GI irritation and bleeding increases. Additive platelet inhibition may prolong bleeding.
management: Monitor for GI symptoms. Curcumin at low-to-moderate doses may actually reduce NSAID-induced GI damage (evidence-based in animal models). However, high-dose curcumin supplements with NSAID therapy require GI monitoring.
references: Aggarwal BB et al. Biochem Pharmacol 2009;78:409–432
Rahmani AH et al. Biomed Res Int 2014;2014:596432

mechanism: Curcumin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-gp, potentially altering chemotherapy pharmacokinetics. In vitro evidence suggests curcumin may antagonise cyclophosphamide activity by reducing alkylation-induced DNA damage through antioxidant mechanisms — potentially reducing chemotherapy efficacy. Conversely, may sensitise some tumour cells to doxorubicin.
effect: Uncertain net effect on chemotherapy efficacy; risk of either antagonism (reducing cell kill) or potentiation depending on tumour type and drug. Until definitive clinical data available, concurrent use during active chemotherapy is not recommended.
management: Advise cancer patients to avoid curcumin supplements during active chemotherapy cycles. Between cycles or in maintenance phase, discuss with oncologist. Culinary turmeric in food is not a concern.
references: Somasundaram S et al. J Biol Chem 2002;277:14645–14656
Howells LM et al. J Nutr Biochem 2007;18:310–318

mechanism: Curcumin activates AMPK pathway and PPAR-γ receptors, improving insulin sensitivity and reducing hepatic gluconeogenesis. Similar mechanism to metformin and thiazolidinediones. Additive glucose-lowering effect documented in clinical trials.
effect: Mild additive hypoglycaemia risk with sulfonylureas and insulin. Clinically, curcumin has shown ability to reduce HbA1c by 0.5–1% in type 2 diabetes — potentially beneficial but requires monitoring.
management: Monitor blood glucose when curcumin supplementation is initiated. May allow modest dose reduction of antidiabetic drug. Particularly useful adjunct in metabolic syndrome management.
references: Chuengsamarn S et al. Diabetes Care 2012;35:2121–2127
Neerati P et al. Phytother Res 2014;28:1796–1800

mechanism: Curcumin chelates iron (Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺) in the GI tract through its β-diketone moiety, forming stable curcumin-iron complexes that are poorly absorbed. Reduces intestinal iron absorption by up to 80% in vitro when taken simultaneously.
effect: Reduced iron bioavailability; may worsen iron deficiency anaemia in at-risk patients (menstruating women, vegetarians, patients on iron therapy).
management: Separate curcumin and iron supplement doses by at least 2–3 hours. Prefer taking iron on empty stomach and curcumin with meals if both required. Monitor haemoglobin and serum ferritin in iron-deficient patients using curcumin.
references: Tuntipopipat S et al. J Nutr 2006;136:2970–2974
Loranthus et al. Food Chem 2014;158:203–209

mechanism: Echinacea's primary pharmacological activity is immunostimulation — activating macrophages, NK cells, T-lymphocytes, and stimulating cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-12). This directly opposes the mechanism of immunosuppressants used to prevent transplant rejection. Concurrent CYP3A4 modulation (weak induction) may reduce cyclosporine levels.
effect: Acute rejection episodes in transplant patients; exacerbation of autoimmune conditions in patients immunosuppressed for connective tissue diseases. Risk of graft-versus-host disease exacerbation.
management: Absolute contraindication in transplant patients and anyone on immunosuppressive therapy for autoimmune disease. Screen all such patients for echinacea use. Duration of immunostimulation may persist 1–2 weeks after discontinuation.
references: Cott J. Altern Ther Health Med 1995;1:58–64
Izzo AA et al. Br J Pharmacol 2004;144:647–650

mechanism: Echinacea extracts contain hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids (trace levels in some preparations) that may cause additive liver injury when combined with known hepatotoxic drugs. Additionally, immunostimulation from echinacea may oppose the immunosuppressive disease-modifying mechanism of methotrexate in rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis.
effect: Potential for hepatotoxicity (elevated liver enzymes, hepatocellular injury) and reduced efficacy of methotrexate immunosuppression in autoimmune disease management.
management: Avoid in patients on methotrexate for RA, psoriasis, or IBD. Monitor LFTs if combination has occurred. Use echinacea preparations certified to be free of pyrrolizidine alkaloids.
references: Miller LG. Arch Intern Med 1998;158:2200–2211

mechanism: Echinacea purpurea inhibits CYP1A2 in vitro and in human pharmacokinetic studies, reducing clearance of CYP1A2 substrates. Caffeine clearance reduced by approximately 30% in crossover studies with 400 mg echinacea TID for 8 days.
effect: Increased plasma levels of CYP1A2 substrates: excessive caffeine effects (insomnia, palpitations, anxiety), theophylline toxicity (nausea, tachyarrhythmias), increased olanzapine/clozapine plasma levels with potential for adverse psychiatric effects.
management: Clinically relevant for patients on narrow-therapeutic-index CYP1A2 substrates (theophylline, clozapine). Monitor theophylline levels. Patients on antipsychotics should notify prescriber if using echinacea.
references: Gorski JC et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2004;75:89–100

mechanism: Echinacea modulates CYP3A4 in a complex manner: intestinal CYP3A4 inhibition (increases oral midazolam AUC ~5%) combined with hepatic CYP3A4 induction (may reduce IV midazolam AUC). Net effect varies by route of administration and dose.
effect: Oral benzodiazepines: modestly increased sedation and anxiolytic effect. IV administration: potentially reduced efficacy in procedural sedation. Generally minor clinical significance.
management: Clinically insignificant at standard echinacea doses in most patients. Note for anaesthesiologists: patients taking echinacea may have altered midazolam pharmacokinetics for pre-operative sedation.
references: Gorski JC et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2004;75:89–100

mechanism: Valerenic acid and its derivatives modulate GABA-A receptors (partial agonists and positive allosteric modulators), enhancing GABAergic inhibitory neurotransmission. Combined with benzodiazepines (which bind to the benzodiazepine-binding site on GABA-A receptor), produces additive CNS depression. Isovaleric acid contributes additional sedative activity.
effect: Excessive CNS depression: excessive sedation, cognitive impairment, psychomotor retardation, respiratory depression risk at high doses, increased fall risk in elderly. Potential for dependence amplification.
management: Advise against simultaneous use, especially in elderly (fall/fracture risk). If both used, reduce benzodiazepine dose by 30–50% and monitor sedation level. Do not combine before driving or operating machinery.
references: Donovan JL et al. Drug Metab Dispos 2004;32:1333–1336
Houghton PJ. J Pharm Pharmacol 1999;51:505–512

mechanism: Barbiturates enhance GABA-A Cl⁻ channel opening (barbiturate binding site); valerenic acid acts as positive allosteric modulator at a distinct site on GABA-A receptor. Dual GABA-A receptor potentiation produces synergistic CNS and respiratory depression. Animal studies show significantly prolonged pentobarbital sleep time with valerian.
effect: Profound CNS depression, respiratory depression, anaesthetic potentiation. Risk of apnea with IV barbiturates in patients who have taken valerian perioperatively. Extended recovery from general anaesthesia.
management: Discontinue valerian at least 2 weeks before elective surgery. Alert anaesthetist to recent valerian use. Reduce induction dose if combination has occurred and monitor respiration closely.
references: Hendriks H et al. Planta Med 1985;51:28–31
Leathwood PD et al. Pharmacol Biochem Behav 1982;17:65–71

mechanism: Valerian's GABAergic mechanism produces additive CNS depression with all other depressant agents. Alcohol itself potentiates GABA-A receptor activity; combined with valerenic acid's allosteric modulation, produces supra-additive sedation. Ethanol may also increase valerian bioavailability.
effect: Excessive sedation, impaired coordination, cognitive impairment, nausea. Risk of accidents, falls. Dangerous when driving or operating machinery. In high doses: respiratory depression risk.
management: Advise patients to avoid alcohol while using valerian. Do not combine with opioids or other CNS depressants. Document in patient counselling notes.
references: Rosecrans JA et al. Arch Int Pharmacodyn Ther 1961;132:294–303

mechanism: Z-drugs act as positive allosteric modulators of GABA-A receptors with preferential selectivity for α1 subunit (sedation). Valerenic acid also modulates GABA-A receptor (β3 subunit preference). Combined GABAergic potentiation produces additive sedation and anxiolysis exceeding either agent alone.
effect: Excessive and prolonged sedation, next-day cognitive impairment ('hangover effect'), rebound anxiety, complex sleep behaviours (somnambulism), amnesia episodes.
management: Avoid concurrent use. If patient insists on using valerian alongside prescribed hypnotic, reduce hypnotic dose and use valerian only on alternating nights. Monitor for complex sleep behaviours.
references: Ortiz JG et al. Neurochem Res 1999;24:1373–1378

mechanism: In vitro studies demonstrate valerian root extract inhibits CYP2C9 with IC50 values in the low micromolar range. Valerenic acid and acetoxyvalerenic acid are likely inhibitory compounds. Human PK studies are limited but suggest potential for clinically relevant interactions with narrow-TI CYP2C9 substrates.
effect: Potential elevation of CYP2C9 substrate plasma levels: warfarin (bleeding risk), phenytoin (toxicity: nystagmus, ataxia), glibenclamide (hypoglycaemia). Clinical significance at standard doses is uncertain but warrants caution.
management: Monitor INR in patients on warfarin if valerian is introduced. Monitor phenytoin levels and drug effects for CYP2C9-metabolised AEDs. Avoid high-dose valerian in patients on narrow-TI CYP2C9 substrates.
references: Lefebvre T et al. J Pharm Pharm Sci 2004;7:265–273

mechanism: Kavalactones (kavain, methysticin, dihydromethysticin) produce GABA-A receptor modulation, Na⁺ channel blockade, and EAA inhibition for CNS depression. Alcohol produces similar GABA-A potentiation and NMDA antagonism. Combined hepatotoxic mechanisms: kavalactones inhibit CYP2E1 — the primary enzyme for alcohol metabolism — causing acetaldehyde accumulation. Both are independently hepatotoxic.
effect: Synergistic hepatotoxicity, with acute liver failure reported (DILI: drug-induced liver injury from combined exposure). Enhanced CNS depression, respiratory depression, and loss of coordination. Disulfiram-like reactions in susceptible individuals.
management: Absolute contraindication. Any patient prescribed or using kava must be explicitly counselled to abstain from alcohol. Monitor LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin) at baseline and monthly during kava use. Liver transplantation reported in severe cases.
references: Escher M et al. BMJ 2001;322:139
Brauer RB et al. Drug Induced Liver Disease 2002;14:40–45

mechanism: Kavalactones modulate GABA-A receptors and inhibit CYP3A4, increasing benzodiazepine plasma levels. Combined GABA-A potentiation from both kava and benzodiazepines produces synergistic CNS depression. Dihydrokawain also inhibits voltage-gated sodium channels.
effect: Profound sedation, coma reported in case reports, respiratory depression. Additive hepatotoxic risk from both agents (benzodiazepines rarely, kava commonly). Severe withdrawal risk if both abruptly discontinued.
management: Avoid combination. In patients already on both, gradual tapering required. Monitor liver function tests. Consider referral to addiction medicine if dependence on both substances has developed.
references: Almeida JC et al. South Med J 1996;89:526–528
Field AC et al. Pharmacol Biochem Behav 2009;93:66–71

mechanism: Kavalactones inhibit dopamine D2 receptors and reduce dopaminergic neurotransmission, directly antagonising the therapeutic mechanism of levodopa in Parkinson's disease. Additionally, kava's dopamine antagonism can precipitate or worsen extrapyramidal symptoms.
effect: Reduced efficacy of levodopa therapy — worsening of Parkinson's disease motor symptoms (increased rigidity, bradykinesia, tremor). Case reports of sudden severe motor deterioration (on-off fluctuation exacerbation) in patients taking kava.
management: Contraindicated in Parkinson's disease patients. Screen all PD patients for kava use. If discovered, discontinue kava and reassess levodopa dosing. Recovery of motor function may take days to weeks.
references: Schelosky L et al. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry 1995;58:639–640

mechanism: Kava is independently associated with hepatotoxicity through multiple mechanisms: CYP2E1 inhibition increasing reactive metabolite burden, glutathione depletion, mitochondrial dysfunction, and possibly immune-mediated hepatocellular necrosis. Concurrent use of any hepatotoxic agent amplifies liver injury risk. With paracetamol: CYP2E1 inhibition paradoxically may initially reduce NAPQI formation but glutathione depletion worsens overall hepatotoxicity.
effect: Acute liver failure, cholestatic hepatitis, fulminant hepatic failure requiring liver transplantation. Over 100 cases of kava-associated hepatotoxicity reported to regulatory agencies in Germany, Switzerland, Australia.
management: Screen patients considering kava for concurrent hepatotoxic drug use. Obtain baseline LFTs. If combination unavoidable, monitor ALT, AST, bilirubin, and INR monthly. Discontinue immediately if ALT >3× ULN. Kava is banned in several countries (Germany, Canada); advise accordingly.
references: Escher M et al. BMJ 2001;322:139
Stickel F et al. J Hepatol 2003;39:62–67
Teschke R et al. Liver Int 2011;31:1429–1436

mechanism: Both kava kavalactones (independent dopamine antagonism) and haloperidol (D2 receptor blockade) reduce dopaminergic transmission in the nigrostriatal and mesolimbic pathways. Additive D2 antagonism produces synergistic extrapyramidal effects. CYP2D6 inhibition by kavalactones may also reduce haloperidol clearance, elevating plasma levels.
effect: Increased extrapyramidal side effects: acute dystonic reactions, drug-induced parkinsonism, akathisia, tardive dyskinesia with long-term use. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) risk if dopaminergic neurotransmission severely impaired.
management: Avoid combination. If inadvertent co-administration, monitor for extrapyramidal symptoms. Treat acute dystonia with anticholinergic (procyclidine, benztropine). Discontinue kava.
references: Mathews JM et al. Drug Metab Dispos 2005;33:1555–1563

mechanism: Kavalactones produce CNS depression through GABA-A modulation and voltage-gated Na⁺ channel blockade, additive with antiepileptic drugs sharing these mechanisms (phenobarbital: GABA-A; valproate: GABA; carbamazepine/phenytoin: Na⁺ channels). Additionally, kava may inhibit CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, raising plasma levels of carbamazepine and phenytoin.
effect: Excessive CNS depression: sedation, cognitive impairment, cerebellar ataxia. Potential for drug toxicity if AED levels elevated. Paradoxically, kava-mediated CYP inhibition could actually improve seizure control (increased AED levels) but at the cost of toxicity.
management: Monitor AED levels if kava is used. Monitor for signs of drug toxicity (nystagmus, ataxia, confusion) and liver function tests. Avoid kava in patients with epilepsy due to unpredictable interactions.
references: Johnson BM et al. Drug Metab Dispos 2003;31:1510–1521

mechanism: Glycyrrhizin and its metabolite glycyrrhetic acid inhibit 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 (11β-HSD2), the enzyme that inactivates cortisol to cortisone in the kidney. This allows cortisol to activate mineralocorticoid receptors, producing pseudoaldosteronism: Na⁺ and water retention, K⁺ wasting, hypertension. This directly opposes the mechanism of antihypertensive drugs.
effect: Severe hypertension (systolic BP elevation of 20–50 mmHg), hypokalaemia (K⁺ <3.0 mmol/L), oedema, and headache. Antihypertensive medications become less effective or ineffective. Doses as low as 100 mg glycyrrhizin/day for several weeks can cause this effect.
management: Warn hypertensive patients to avoid licorice root supplementation. DGL (deglycyrrhizinated licorice) removes ≥97% of glycyrrhizin and is much safer. Monitor BP and serum potassium. Discontinue licorice; recovery takes 3–4 weeks.
references: Stewart PM et al. Lancet 1987;2:821–824
Walker BR et al. Hypertension 1994;24:619–624

mechanism: Glycyrrhizin-induced pseudoaldosteronism causes hypokalaemia through renal K⁺ wasting. Hypokalaemia increases myocardial sensitivity to digoxin by increasing digoxin binding to Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase (the therapeutic target), markedly lowering the digoxin toxic threshold. Toxicity occurs at normally therapeutic digoxin plasma levels.
effect: Digoxin toxicity: nausea, vomiting, visual disturbances (green-yellow halos, blurred vision), bradyarrhythmias, heart block, ventricular arrhythmias. Life-threatening cardiac toxicity at standard therapeutic digoxin levels if patient is hypokalaemic.
management: Contraindicated. Monitor serum potassium and digoxin levels in patients taking any licorice-containing products. Maintain K⁺ >4.0 mmol/L in all digitalis patients. Consider DGL substitution.
references: Harada T et al. Nihon Naika Gakkai Zasshi 2002;91:1960–1962
Pelliccia F et al. BMJ Case Rep 2014;2014:bcr2013202568

mechanism: Both loop diuretics and thiazides cause renal potassium wasting through enhanced distal tubular Na⁺/K⁺ exchange. Glycyrrhizin's 11β-HSD2 inhibition independently causes hypokalaemia via aldosterone-like effect on principal cells. Additive K⁺ depletion through mechanistically distinct pathways.
effect: Severe hypokalaemia (K⁺ <2.5 mmol/L), hypomagnesaemia, metabolic alkalosis. Risk of cardiac arrhythmias (torsades de pointes, ventricular fibrillation) and rhabdomyolysis.
management: Avoid combination. If patient must take licorice medicinally, supplement potassium and magnesium. Monitor electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺) monthly. Switch to DGL formulation if possible.
references: Mantero F et al. Clin Exp Pharmacol Physiol 1989;16:593–598

mechanism: Glycyrrhetic acid inhibits 11β-HSD2, reducing cortisol inactivation, and inhibits 5α/5β-reductases, slowing corticosteroid metabolism. Combined with exogenous corticosteroids, produces additive mineralocorticoid and glucocorticoid effects. Some evidence of corticosteroid half-life prolongation.
effect: Cushingoid features: hypertension, hypokalaemia, oedema, hyperglycaemia, weight gain, skin changes. Enhanced corticosteroid toxicity: HPA axis suppression, osteoporosis risk with long-term combined use.
management: Avoid high-dose licorice in patients on systemic corticosteroids. DGL is safer and preferred. Monitor BP, blood glucose, and electrolytes. Inform prescribing physician of all licorice supplement use.
references: Kageyama Y et al. Intern Med 1992;31:1205–1208

mechanism: Glycyrrhizin inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, reducing warfarin clearance and potentially increasing INR. Additionally, licorice-induced hypokalaemia may indirectly affect coagulation factor synthesis. Some licorice preparations contain compounds with weak coumarins.
effect: INR elevation with increased bleeding risk. Conversely, some evidence that large amounts of licorice may decrease warfarin effect. Net effect is unpredictable and patient-specific.
management: Monitor INR closely in anticoagulated patients using licorice supplements. Avoid large amounts. If BP elevation or hypokalaemia develop, prioritise these as the more immediate safety concern.
references: Takanaga H et al. J Pharm Pharmacol 2000;52:1065–1074

mechanism: Withanolides and withanosides in ashwagandha interact with GABA-A receptors as positive allosteric modulators. Sitoindoside VII and VIII exert anxiolytic effects similar to GABAergic drugs. Combined with prescribed CNS depressants, produces additive sedation. CYP3A4 inhibition by withanolides may also raise plasma levels of benzodiazepines.
effect: Excessive sedation, psychomotor impairment, cognitive slowing, respiratory depression risk at high doses, increased fall risk.
management: Advise caution when combining with prescribed sedatives. Reduce ashwagandha dose or reduce sedative drug dose with medical supervision. Avoid before driving. Particularly relevant in elderly patients.
references: Mehta AK et al. Phytomedicine 1991;2:127–136
Choudhary D et al. AYU 2017;38:78–83

mechanism: Ashwagandha stimulates thyroid hormone synthesis and secretion, increasing T3 and T4 production. Animal studies demonstrate significant increase in T3 and T4 levels with ashwagandha root extract. Human study (Sharma et al. 2018) confirmed elevated TSH, T3, T4 in subclinical hypothyroid patients.
effect: In patients on levothyroxine: thyrotoxicosis symptoms (palpitations, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, anxiety) due to additive thyroid hormone elevation. In patients on antithyroid drugs for Graves' disease: ashwagandha may counteract antithyroid therapy.
management: Monitor thyroid function tests (TSH, free T3, free T4) when ashwagandha is introduced. Avoid in thyrotoxicosis, Graves' disease. May be beneficial in hypothyroidism but requires dose monitoring of levothyroxine. Contraindicated with antithyroid drugs.
references: Sharma AK et al. J Altern Complement Med 2018;24:243–248
Panda S et al. J Pharm Pharmacol 1998;50:1065–1068

mechanism: Withanolides are potent immunomodulators: enhance NK cell activity, macrophage phagocytosis, lymphocyte proliferation, and cytokine production (IL-2, IFN-γ). This immunostimulatory activity directly opposes immunosuppressive drugs used for transplantation and autoimmune diseases.
effect: Potential for transplant rejection, exacerbation of autoimmune conditions (RA, SLE, IBD), or reduced efficacy of biologic therapy. Risk proportional to dose and immunosuppression depth.
management: Avoid in transplant patients and those on immunosuppressive therapy for autoimmune disease. Advise disclosure of all herbal use to transplant physicians and rheumatologists.
references: Davis L et al. J Ethnopharmacol 2001;77:275–283

mechanism: Withanolides improve insulin sensitivity by activating PPAR-γ pathways, reducing hepatic gluconeogenesis, and improving peripheral glucose uptake. Clinical studies show HbA1c reduction of 0.5–1.5% in type 2 diabetes patients. Additive hypoglycaemic mechanism with antidiabetic medications.
effect: Mild hypoglycaemia risk with sulfonylureas or insulin; beneficial effect with metformin. Clinical data suggests ashwagandha may reduce FBS by 10–15 mg/dL.
management: Monitor blood glucose when initiating ashwagandha. May allow dose reduction of antidiabetic drug — adjust under supervision. Particularly valuable as adjunct in metabolic syndrome management.
references: Anwer T et al. Acta Pol Pharm 2008;65:537–542
Raut AA et al. J Ayurveda Integr Med 2012;3:111–114

mechanism: Ashwagandha reduces blood pressure through multiple mechanisms: adaptogenic reduction of cortisol (reducing sympathetic activation), GABA-A modulation reducing vascular sympathetic tone, and possible mild ACE inhibition by withanolides.
effect: Additive blood pressure lowering, risk of orthostatic hypotension and dizziness. Beneficial in most contexts but may cause symptomatic hypotension in patients on maximal antihypertensive doses.
management: Monitor blood pressure when ashwagandha is initiated. May provide complementary benefit in stress-related hypertension. Educate patient on position changes and hypotension symptoms.
references: Cooley K et al. PLoS One 2009;4:e6628

mechanism: Bacosides A and B in Bacopa monnieri inhibit acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and enhance synaptic acetylcholine availability, serving as cholinomimetics. This directly antagonises the mechanism of anticholinergic drugs (competitive antagonists at muscarinic receptors). Pharmacodynamic opposition.
effect: Reduced efficacy of anticholinergic therapy: poor bladder control in OAB patients, recurrence of Parkinson's tremor if anticholinergics were prescribed for that. Conversely, in patients on anticholinergics for GI spasm: Bacopa may restore peristalsis (beneficial or disruptive depending on indication).
management: Monitor therapeutic response to anticholinergic medication when Bacopa is used concurrently. May need dose adjustment of anticholinergic. Beneficial interaction in dementia where anticholinergic burden is being minimised.
references: Rao AV et al. Phytomedicine 2006;13:677–687
Bhattacharya SK et al. Phytomedicine 2000;7:207–212

mechanism: Bacopa may modulate CYP3A4 activity; bacosides have demonstrated anticonvulsant properties in animal models, potentially through Ca²⁺ channel modulation and GABAergic enhancement. Combined effects with AEDs: may potentiate CNS depression or alter plasma drug levels through enzyme modulation. Phenytoin pharmacokinetics may be altered.
effect: Potential phenytoin toxicity (elevated levels) or reduced seizure control (reduced levels). CNS depression additive with phenobarbital and carbamazepine. Animal studies also suggest Bacopa reduces carbamazepine efficacy in some seizure models.
management: Monitor AED plasma levels if Bacopa is introduced. Monitor seizure frequency. Consult neurologist before initiating Bacopa in epileptic patients.
references: Sahoo N et al. J Ethnopharmacol 2010;128:339–344

mechanism: Bacosides modulate voltage-dependent Ca²⁺ channels in neuronal cells; some evidence of peripheral vascular smooth muscle Ca²⁺ channel modulation. Additive vasodilatory effect possible when combined with dihydropyridine CCBs. CYP3A4 modulation may affect CCB plasma levels.
effect: Enhanced antihypertensive effect, dizziness, ankle oedema. Generally mild and clinically manageable.
management: Monitor blood pressure when Bacopa supplementation is initiated. Usually not clinically significant at standard doses. Educate patient on orthostatic precautions.
references: Rai D et al. Phytomedicine 2003;10:523–527

mechanism: Bacopa monnieri extract has been shown in animal studies to stimulate thyroid gland hormone synthesis and increase T4 secretion by approximately 41%. The mechanism involves direct stimulation of thyroid peroxidase activity. May interact with levothyroxine in additive manner.
effect: Potential for subclinical or overt thyrotoxicosis in patients on levothyroxine replacement: palpitations, weight loss, increased sweating, anxiety.
management: Monitor TSH and free T4 when Bacopa is initiated in hypothyroid patients on replacement therapy. May require levothyroxine dose reduction. Avoid in thyrotoxicosis.
references: Kar A et al. J Ethnopharmacol 2002;81:281–285

herbAliases: Terminalia chebula + T. bellerica + Emblica officinalis
mechanism: Triphala contains tannic acids and ellagitannins (chebulagic acid, chebulinic acid from Haritaki; gallic acid from Amalaki) that inhibit platelet aggregation. Emblicanin A and B from Amalaki have antioxidant and antiplatelet properties. Gallic acid demonstrates CYP2C9 inhibitory activity in vitro. Combined pharmacodynamic antiplatelet and possible pharmacokinetic interaction with warfarin.
effect: Increased bleeding tendency, potential INR elevation. Risk amplified in patients with low Vitamin K intake.
management: Monitor INR in anticoagulated patients using Triphala. High-dose supplementation (>2g/day) poses greater risk than traditional Ayurvedic formulations at standard doses. Educate patient to use consistently and report bruising.
references: Dwivedi S et al. Phytomedicine 2014;21:1147–1154
Yogananth N et al. J Nat Prod 2009;2:196–200

mechanism: Triphala constituents inhibit α-glucosidase and α-amylase, reducing postprandial glucose absorption. Gallic acid activates PPAR-γ improving insulin sensitivity. Emblicanin from Amalaki enhances insulin secretion. Additive glucose-lowering effect with antidiabetic medications.
effect: Additive reduction in postprandial blood glucose and HbA1c. Risk of hypoglycaemia with sulfonylureas and insulin if doses not adjusted.
management: Monitor fasting blood glucose and 2-hour postprandial glucose when Triphala is initiated. May allow dose reduction of antidiabetic drug. Beneficial adjunct in type 2 diabetes management.
references: Sabu MC et al. J Ethnopharmacol 2002;81:155–160
Patil VV et al. J Cell Tissue Res 2009;9:1921

mechanism: High tannin content (tannic acid, chebulinic acid, galloylglucose) in Triphala chelates divalent metal cations (Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺, Zn²⁺, Ca²⁺) in the GI tract, forming insoluble complexes that reduce mineral bioavailability. Amalaki's vitamin C content partially counteracts iron chelation for iron specifically (vitamin C reduces Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺, improving absorption).
effect: Reduced iron absorption (potentially relevant in iron deficiency anaemia), reduced zinc absorption. However, Amalaki's ascorbate content may partially mitigate iron chelation.
management: Separate Triphala and iron/mineral supplement doses by at least 3 hours. Take iron on empty stomach, Triphala with/after meals. Monitor haemoglobin and ferritin in anaemic patients using Triphala.
references: Kumari K et al. Indian J Pharm Sci 2008;70:657–662

mechanism: Triphala demonstrates immunomodulatory activity: chebulinic acid and ellagic acid activate macrophages, NK cells, and T-lymphocytes. This immunostimulation may oppose immunosuppressive therapy. Additionally, gallic acid inhibits P-glycoprotein, potentially altering cyclosporine or tacrolimus absorption.
effect: Potential for reduced immunosuppressive efficacy; risk of acute rejection in transplant patients. P-gp inhibition may elevate calcineurin inhibitor levels unpredictably.
management: Avoid in transplant patients and those on immunosuppressive therapy for autoimmune conditions. Monitor drug levels if inadvertent use has occurred.
references: Srikumar R et al. Phytomedicine 2007;14:44–52

mechanism: Fenugreek seeds contain coumarins (scopoletin, 3β-hydroxy-Δ5-steroids) with anti-vitamin K activity. Additionally, saponins and mucilage slow GI transit, altering drug absorption. Steroidal saponins may weakly inhibit CYP2C9. Pharmacodynamic anticoagulant interaction through independent mechanism.
effect: INR elevation with increased bleeding risk. Case report of INR elevation from 2.0 to 4.7 after initiation of fenugreek seed therapy. Fenugreek also has antiplatelet effects (inhibits TXA2 synthesis).
management: Monitor INR closely if patient uses fenugreek supplements. Advise consistent use (start/stop changes are most dangerous). High culinary doses (common in Indian cooking) warrant monitoring in anticoagulated patients.
references: Lambert JP et al. Ann Pharmacother 2001;35:1174–1177

mechanism: Multiple mechanisms: 4-hydroxyisoleucine directly stimulates insulin secretion from pancreatic β-cells; soluble fibre (galactomannan) slows glucose absorption; trigonelline inhibits gluconeogenesis and improves GLUT4 translocation. Human studies demonstrate HbA1c reduction of 0.5–2% and FBS reduction of 10–25 mg/dL.
effect: Significant additive hypoglycaemia risk with sulfonylureas and insulin. Enhanced and more rapid response to antidiabetic medications. Risk of symptomatic hypoglycaemia.
management: Monitor blood glucose closely when fenugreek is added to antidiabetic regimen. Dose reduction of sulfonylurea or insulin may be needed. Beneficial and evidence-based adjunct but requires monitoring. Particularly effective in South Asian patients with T2DM.
references: Gupta A et al. J Assoc Physicians India 2001;49:1057–1061
Neelakantan N et al. Nutr J 2014;13:7

mechanism: Fenugreek seeds have very high fibre content (galactomannan) and tannin content that can bind iron in the GI tract, reducing its absorption. Phytate content also chelates divalent cations including Fe²⁺.
effect: Reduced iron bioavailability, potentially worsening iron deficiency anaemia. Particularly relevant in pregnant women or anaemic patients who rely on both traditional diet (high fenugreek use in Indian cuisine) and iron supplementation.
management: Separate iron supplement from fenugreek by at least 2 hours. Monitor haemoglobin and ferritin. Fenugreek seeds consumed at meal times (culinary use) will impair iron absorption from that meal — iron supplement should be taken away from main meals in such patients.
references: Tuntipopipat S et al. J Nutr 2006;136:2970–2974

mechanism: Fenugreek seed galactomannan fibre can bind levothyroxine in the GI tract if taken simultaneously, reducing oral bioavailability. Levothyroxine is known to be sensitive to food and supplement interactions affecting absorption.
effect: Reduced levothyroxine plasma levels, inadequate thyroid hormone replacement, symptoms of hypothyroidism (fatigue, constipation, weight gain, cold intolerance).
management: Take levothyroxine at least 2 hours before or 4 hours after fenugreek supplementation. Monitor TSH 6–8 weeks after introducing fenugreek. A very common interaction given fenugreek's widespread use in South Asian populations.
references: Benvenga S et al. Thyroid 2008;18:293–301

mechanism: Black cohosh contains triterpene glycosides (actein, 26-deoxyactein, cimicifugoside) with complex oestrogen receptor activity — evidence shows both agonist activity (ERβ) and possible ERα antagonism. Concurrent CYP2D6 inhibition by black cohosh reduces tamoxifen conversion to active endoxifen metabolite. In breast cancer: ERβ agonism may promote tumour growth; CYP2D6 inhibition reduces tamoxifen efficacy.
effect: Reduced tamoxifen efficacy (lower endoxifen levels) in breast cancer patients, risking tumour recurrence. Potential for oestrogen-mediated tumour stimulation in hormone receptor-positive breast cancer.
management: Contraindicated in breast cancer patients, particularly those on tamoxifen therapy. Screen all oncology patients for black cohosh use. Black cohosh use during breast cancer treatment is not supported by evidence and carries significant risk.
references: Brasky TM et al. Breast Cancer Res Treat 2010;122:865–874
Stearns V et al. J Natl Cancer Inst 2003;95:1758–1764

mechanism: Black cohosh is independently associated with hepatotoxicity (case reports of acute liver failure, autoimmune hepatitis, fulminant hepatic failure), likely through reactive metabolite formation via CYP3A4 oxidation. Combined with hepatotoxic drugs, risk of additive liver injury is increased through independent toxic mechanisms.
effect: Hepatocellular injury, cholestatic hepatitis, fulminant hepatic failure. Risk appears idiosyncratic rather than dose-dependent, making prediction difficult.
management: Monitor LFTs (ALT, AST) at baseline and every 3 months in patients using black cohosh. Avoid combination with known hepatotoxic drugs. Discontinue immediately if ALT >3× ULN. Regulatory agencies (MHRA, TGA) require hepatotoxicity warning on all black cohosh products.
references: Levitsky J et al. Dig Dis Sci 2005;50:538–539
Whiting PW et al. Med J Aust 2002;177:440–443

mechanism: Formononetin and other isoflavone-like compounds in some black cohosh species may produce weak vasodilatory effects. Triterpene glycosides may modulate autonomic vasomotor control. Additive hypotensive pharmacodynamics.
effect: Mild additional blood pressure lowering, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension. Generally not clinically significant at standard doses.
management: Monitor blood pressure when black cohosh is initiated. Educate patient on position changes. More relevant in postmenopausal women (primary users) who are often on antihypertensives.
references: Jarry H et al. Maturitas 2003;46(suppl 1):S67–S77

mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition by black cohosh constituents may reduce simvastatin and lovastatin clearance (CYP3A4 substrates), potentially increasing statin plasma levels. Combined hepatotoxic risk from both agents is an independent concern.
effect: Elevated statin plasma levels: myopathy, rhabdomyolysis risk (particularly with simvastatin/lovastatin). Additive hepatotoxic risk. Pravastatin and rosuvastatin (non-CYP3A4 substrates) pose less pharmacokinetic risk.
management: Prefer non-CYP3A4 metabolised statins (pravastatin, rosuvastatin) if combination unavoidable. Monitor CK levels and LFTs. Advise patient to report muscle pain or weakness immediately.
references: Gurley BJ et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2005;77:415–426

mechanism: Parthenolide (the primary bioactive sesquiterpene lactone in feverfew) inhibits platelet aggregation by inhibiting thromboxane A2 synthesis, suppressing NF-κB, and reducing platelet granule secretion. Additive antiplatelet pharmacodynamics with warfarin's anticoagulant effect. Also possible weak COX-1 inhibition.
effect: Increased bleeding risk; INR may be artificially stable (warfarin effect unchanged) but platelet-dependent haemostasis is impaired. Perioperative bleeding risk elevated.
management: Advise patients on warfarin to avoid feverfew supplements. Discontinue at least 7 days before surgery. Monitor INR and assess platelet function if combination occurs.
references: Murphy JJ et al. Lancet 1988;2:189–192
Heck AM et al. Am J Health Syst Pharm 2000;57:1221–1227

mechanism: Parthenolide inhibits arachidonic acid-mediated platelet aggregation via TXA2 suppression, acting through a COX-1 pathway that partially overlaps with aspirin. When combined with aspirin, there is additive (and potentially synergistic) antiplatelet effect. Clopidogrel combination provides dual antiplatelet pathway blockade.
effect: Enhanced antiplatelet effect leading to increased bleeding risk: epistaxis, GI bleeding, easy bruising, prolonged wound bleeding. Risk amplified on dual antiplatelet therapy.
management: Avoid in patients already on antiplatelet therapy. Discontinue 7 days before any surgical procedure. Educate patient that feverfew supplements affect platelet function similarly to aspirin.
references: Groenewegen WA et al. J R Soc Med 1986;79:654–655

mechanism: NSAIDs inhibit COX-1 and COX-2; parthenolide inhibits NF-κB and may reduce COX-2 expression. Pharmacodynamic overlap in anti-inflammatory mechanisms. Additionally, NSAIDs may reduce feverfew efficacy: in vitro evidence suggests ibuprofen blocks parthenolide's anti-inflammatory actions.
effect: Two competing effects: (1) additive anti-inflammatory benefit for migraine (if using NSAIDs as abortive therapy) but (2) NSAIDs may paradoxically reduce feverfew's prophylactic efficacy. Also additive GI mucosal irritation.
management: Use NSAIDs only for acute migraine, not concurrent with long-term feverfew prophylaxis. Monitor for GI symptoms. Separate doses if both are needed.
references: Pattrick M et al. Ann Rheum Dis 1989;48:547–549

mechanism: Parthenolide inhibits platelet serotonin release (serotonin is stored in platelet dense granules and released upon activation; parthenolide inhibits granule secretion). Combined with SSRIs depleting platelet serotonin stores, this may produce additive platelet serotonin depletion. Also: weak serotonin antagonism by parthenolide at 5-HT2A receptors may theoretically oppose SSRI effect.
effect: Additive platelet dysfunction — increased bleeding risk (particularly GI bleeding, which is already elevated with SSRIs alone). Potential for reduced migraine prophylaxis efficacy if serotonergic migraine mechanisms are targeted.
management: Monitor for signs of GI bleeding in patients on SSRIs who take feverfew. Add gastroprotection (PPI) if also using NSAIDs. Generally minor clinical significance.
references: Groenewegen WA et al. J R Soc Med 1986;79:654–655

mechanism: Hyperforin induces CYP3A4, which is responsible for the metabolism of simvastatin, lovastatin, and to a lesser extent atorvastatin. Simvastatin AUC reduced by approximately 50% after 14 days of SJW administration. Pravastatin and rosuvastatin (not CYP3A4 substrates) are not significantly affected.
effect: Reduced statin plasma levels leading to inadequate LDL-cholesterol lowering and failure of cardiovascular risk reduction. Conversely, if SJW is abruptly discontinued, statin levels may rise causing myopathy.
management: If patient on simvastatin or atorvastatin wishes to use SJW, switch to pravastatin or rosuvastatin which are not significantly affected by CYP3A4 induction. Monitor lipid profile at 6–8 weeks.
references: Sugimoto K et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2001;70:518–524

mechanism: Ginkgo flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, isorhamnetin) demonstrate monoamine oxidase inhibitory activity in vitro — inhibiting both MAO-A and MAO-B. Combined with prescribed MAOIs, may produce additive enzyme inhibition with unpredictable serotonergic and noradrenergic effects. Risk of hypertensive crisis from norepinephrine excess.
effect: Hypertensive crisis, serotonin syndrome (if MAO-A is inhibited), excessive noradrenergic activation. Risk is theoretical but pharmacologically plausible.
management: Caution in patients on MAOIs for depression or Parkinson's disease (rasagiline, selegiline). Monitor blood pressure and neurological symptoms. Avoid high-dose ginkgo.
references: White HL et al. Biochem Pharmacol 1996;52:1033–1039

mechanism: Kavalactones (particularly methysticin and dihydromethysticin) inhibit CYP3A4 in the intestinal wall and liver. This increases bioavailability and reduces clearance of CYP3A4 substrates with narrow therapeutic indices.
effect: Elevated plasma levels of CYP3A4 substrates: cyclosporine toxicity (nephrotoxicity), benzodiazepine toxicity (excessive sedation), opioid toxicity (respiratory depression), vinca alkaloid toxicity.
management: Avoid in patients on any narrow-TI CYP3A4 substrate. Monitor drug levels and adverse effects. Kava's CYP3A4 inhibition may persist for several days after discontinuation.
references: Mathews JM et al. Drug Metab Dispos 2005;33:1555–1563
Zou L et al. Planta Med 2004;70:129–135

mechanism: 6-Gingerol has been shown to bind 5-HT3 receptors (antiemetic mechanism) and weakly modulate serotonin activity. Combined with SSRIs, theoretical potential for mild additive serotonergic effect. The pharmacokinetic contribution via CYP2D6 is minimal at culinary doses.
effect: Weak additive serotonergic effects at very high supplement doses — mild nausea, diarrhoea, restlessness. Clinically significant only at very high ginger extract doses (>5g/day). Standard culinary use is safe.
management: Clinically insignificant at normal supplement doses. Only relevant with very high-dose ginger extracts. No routine monitoring required.
references: Lumb AB. Anaesthesia 1994;49:1114–1115

mechanism: Withanosides enhance GABAergic tone through GABA-A receptor modulation. Animal studies demonstrate that ashwagandha significantly prolongs thiopentone-induced sleep time, indicating potentiation of barbiturate CNS depression. Triethylene glycol (constituent) has independent sleep-inducing properties.
effect: Prolonged anaesthesia recovery, excessive CNS depression, respiratory depression risk. Extended surgical anaesthesia duration unpredictably.
management: Advise patients to discontinue ashwagandha at least 2 weeks before elective surgery. Alert anaesthesiologist to recent use. May require reduced induction dose.
references: Mohan L et al. Phytomedicine 2006;13:650–655

mechanism: Glycyrrhizin has oestrogenic activity (binds oestrogen receptors) and inhibits 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase involved in oestrogen metabolism. May elevate oestrogen levels, increasing thromboembolic risk when combined with OCP. Additionally, fluid retention from mineralocorticoid effects may be amplified by oestrogen's effect on renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.
effect: Increased risk of thromboembolic events (DVT, PE, stroke) when oestrogenic activity of licorice combines with OCP-induced procoagulant state. Hypertension, oedema, and hypokalaemia risk amplified.
management: Advise women on hormonal contraceptives to avoid high-dose licorice root supplements. DGL is safer alternative. Monitor blood pressure.
references: De Klerk GJ et al. BMJ 1997;314:731–732

mechanism: Animal studies suggest Bacopa modulates opioid receptor sensitivity and may enhance morphine analgesia through cholinergic enhancement. Acetylcholine plays a role in opioid analgesia modulation. Bacopa's AChE inhibition may amplify opioid-mediated pain control while potentially increasing adverse effects.
effect: Mild potentiation of opioid effects: enhanced analgesia (possibly beneficial) but also increased sedation, constipation, and respiratory depression risk.
management: Monitor for enhanced opioid effects. Dose reduction of opioid may be possible with Bacopa adjunct therapy. Generally minor clinical significance at standard doses.
references: Bhattacharya SK et al. Phytother Res 2000;14:592–595

mechanism: Curcumin stimulates bile secretion and increases gastric mucus production. In some patients, high-dose curcumin supplements can cause dyspepsia, nausea, and GI discomfort, potentially worsening GERD or gastritis that PPIs are treating. Additionally, alkaline GI pH induced by antacids may alter curcumin absorption.
effect: Reduced curcumin absorption in alkaline environment; potential for dyspepsia at high curcumin doses opposing antacid/PPI therapy.
management: Take curcumin with food to minimise GI irritation. Avoid very high-dose curcumin supplements in patients with active GERD or peptic ulcer. Standard doses (500–1000 mg/day) are generally well tolerated.
references: Epelbaum R et al. Oncology 2010;78:65–68

mechanism: Ginkgo modulates CYP3A4 activity (net inhibitory effect in some studies). Combined with benzodiazepines metabolised by CYP3A4, may modestly reduce clearance and increase plasma benzodiazepine levels. Effect is generally minor and dose-dependent.
effect: Mild increase in benzodiazepine sedative effects: increased drowsiness, cognitive slowing. Usually clinically insignificant at standard supplement doses.
management: No routine monitoring required at standard doses. Caution with high-dose ginkgo in patients on high-dose benzodiazepines. Advise patient about potential additive sedation.
references: Yin OQ et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2004;76:654–663

mechanism: Both feverfew (via parthenolide's serotonin antagonism and platelet inhibition) and ergotamine (via 5-HT1B/1D partial agonism and vasoconstriction) are used in migraine. Theoretical additive serotonergic vasoconstriction when combined. Feverfew may also reduce ergotamine efficacy through serotonin receptor antagonism.
effect: Possible additive peripheral vasoconstriction, cold extremities. Potential for ergotamine analgesia reduction. Risk of serotonergic cardiovascular effects (coronary vasospasm).
management: Avoid concurrent use as acute abortive ergotamine therapy with chronic feverfew prophylaxis. Use triptans as abortive therapy in patients on feverfew prophylaxis.
references: Johnson ES et al. BMJ 1985;291:569–573

mechanism: In vitro studies demonstrate valerian root inhibits CYP3A4. Human PK study (Donovan et al. 2004) found no significant effect on midazolam AUC, suggesting in vivo inhibition is less clinically relevant than in vitro data. High-dose extracts may have greater effect.
effect: Generally clinically insignificant. At very high valerian doses, modest elevation of CYP3A4 substrates possible. Greater relevance in patients on narrow-TI CYP3A4 substrates (cyclosporine, tacrolimus).
management: No routine monitoring required at standard valerian doses. Caution with high-dose extracts (>900 mg/day) in patients on narrow-TI CYP3A4 substrates.
references: Donovan JL et al. Drug Metab Dispos 2004;32:1333–1336

mechanism: Echinacea polysaccharides (arabinogalactans, echinacoside) stimulate macrophage activation, NK cell cytotoxicity, and production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6). Biologic agents (anti-TNF, anti-CD20) suppress specific arms of the immune system to treat autoimmune conditions. Echinacea-mediated cytokine stimulation may directly oppose the mechanism of action of biologics.
effect: Risk of reduced biologic efficacy; immune stimulation may trigger disease flare in RA, Crohn's disease, psoriasis, or other autoimmune conditions managed with biologics.
management: Contraindicated in patients on biologic therapy for autoimmune disease. Screen all rheumatology and gastroenterology patients for echinacea use.
references: Percival SS. J Am Diet Assoc 2000;100:1368–1380

mechanism: Garlic constituents modulate CYP3A4 activity (induction at high doses, inhibition at lower doses) and CYP2B6, which is the primary metabolic pathway for efavirenz. Unpredictable effects on NNRTI plasma levels. Some studies show modest reduction in efavirenz AUC.
effect: Subtherapeutic NNRTI levels risking virologic failure, or elevated levels causing neuropsychiatric toxicity (vivid dreams, dizziness, cognitive impairment with efavirenz).
management: Avoid high-dose garlic supplements in HIV patients on NNRTI-based regimens. Dietary garlic at normal culinary amounts is less likely to cause significant interactions. Monitor HIV viral load.
references: Tsai HH et al. Ann Pharmacother 2012;46:e8

mechanism: In vitro studies demonstrate withanolides inhibit CYP3A4. Human pharmacokinetic data show modest CYP3A4 inhibitory effects. Withanolides A and D have demonstrated enzyme inhibitory activity. Clinical significance depends on dose and individual CYP3A4 genetic polymorphism.
effect: Modest elevation of CYP3A4 substrate plasma levels. Unlikely to cause significant toxicity at standard ashwagandha doses (300–600 mg) but may amplify drug effects.
management: Monitor for enhanced drug effects with narrow-TI CYP3A4 substrates. No routine monitoring needed at standard ashwagandha doses. Advise patients to disclose use to prescribing physicians.
references: Ulbricht C et al. Am J Health Syst Pharm 2015;72:1282–1292

mechanism: Triphala's high tannin content chelates metal cofactors (Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺, Fe²⁺) required for quinolone (ciprofloxacin) and tetracycline absorption. The mucilaginous fibre content may further impair antibiotic GI absorption by forming a physical barrier. Triphala also modulates gut microbiome which may interact with antibiotic-mediated dysbiosis.
effect: Reduced antibiotic plasma levels, potentially inadequate treatment of infection. Subtherapeutic ciprofloxacin or tetracycline levels risk treatment failure and antibiotic resistance selection.
management: Separate Triphala and antibiotic doses by at least 2–3 hours (take antibiotic on empty stomach, Triphala with/after meals). This is particularly important for fluoroquinolones and tetracyclines.
references: Izzo AA et al. Fundam Clin Pharmacol 2010;24:413–428

mechanism: Glycyrrhizin's mineralocorticoid-like effects elevate cortisol levels (via 11β-HSD2 inhibition), and cortisol antagonises insulin action on peripheral tissues (glucose uptake) and stimulates hepatic gluconeogenesis. This directly opposes antidiabetic therapy. Compounding hypokalaemia (from pseudoaldosteronism) further impairs insulin secretion (K⁺ required for β-cell depolarisation).
effect: Poorly controlled blood glucose in diabetic patients; requirement for higher antidiabetic drug doses. Risk amplified with high licorice intake over weeks.
management: Advise diabetic patients to avoid licorice root supplements. DGL is safer. Monitor FBS and HbA1c. Consider DGL as alternative if licorice is used medicinally (e.g., for peptic ulcer).
references: Chen MF et al. Arch Dermatol Res 1991;283:209–212

mechanism: Voriconazole and itraconazole are primarily CYP3A4 substrates; SJW-mediated CYP3A4 and P-gp induction dramatically reduces their plasma levels. Voriconazole is also a CYP2C19 substrate — dual induction of CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 produces particularly severe reductions in voriconazole exposure (>70% reduction in some studies).
effect: Subtherapeutic antifungal levels, failure to clear serious fungal infections (aspergillosis, candidaemia), risk of treatment failure and mortality in immunocompromised patients.
management: Contraindicated in patients on antifungal triazoles, particularly voriconazole. Monitor antifungal therapeutic drug monitoring levels if combination has occurred. Discontinue SJW immediately.
references: Rengelshausen J et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2005;77:188–197

mechanism: Ginkgo inhibits CYP1A2 in vitro and in pharmacokinetic studies. Tizanidine is a CYP1A2 substrate with a very narrow therapeutic index — even modest CYP1A2 inhibition can significantly raise its plasma levels. Ginkgo may reduce tizanidine clearance by 40–60%.
effect: Elevated tizanidine levels causing severe hypotension, sedation, bradycardia. Tizanidine toxicity has caused hospitalisations with CYP1A2 inhibitors. Similar risk with theophylline (toxicity: seizures, arrhythmias) and clozapine (sedation, metabolic effects).
management: Avoid ginkgo in patients on tizanidine or other narrow-TI CYP1A2 substrates. Monitor for tizanidine toxicity symptoms. If combination unavoidable, reduce tizanidine dose significantly.
references: Gorski JC et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2004;75:89–100

mechanism: Kava depletes hepatic glutathione (GSH) stores through kavalactone-mediated oxidative stress and direct GSH conjugation. Paracetamol hepatotoxicity is mediated by NAPQI (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine) — a reactive metabolite normally detoxified by hepatic GSH. When GSH is depleted by kava, NAPQI accumulates causing centrilobular hepatocellular necrosis. CYP2E1 inhibition by kava may initially reduce NAPQI formation but overall GSH depletion increases vulnerability.
effect: Acute hepatocellular necrosis at paracetamol doses that would normally be safe. Particularly dangerous in patients taking paracetamol at maximum recommended doses (3–4 g/day) while using kava regularly. Liver failure and requirement for transplantation documented.
management: Contraindicated. All patients using kava must be counselled to limit paracetamol use to minimum doses and shortest duration. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) should be initiated early if paracetamol toxicity is suspected in a kava user.
references: Stickel F et al. J Hepatol 2003;39:62–67
Clouatre DL. Toxicol Lett 2004;150:85–96

mechanism: Fenugreek's antiplatelet activity (TXA2 inhibition by saponins, coumarin compounds) combined with LMWH anticoagulation produces pharmacodynamic synergy in the haemostatic system — impairing both coagulation cascade (heparin) and primary haemostasis (fenugreek antiplatelet effect).
