effect: Increased haemorrhagic complications in patients on therapeutic anticoagulation for DVT/PE treatment or post-surgical prophylaxis. Bruising, wound haematoma, increased drainage.
management: Discontinue fenugreek supplements when initiating LMWH therapy. Monitor for signs of bleeding. Educate patients undergoing total joint replacement or abdominal surgery who use fenugreek medicinally.
references: Bordia A et al. Prostaglandins Leukot Essent Fatty Acids 1997;56:379–384

mechanism: Curcumin chelates divalent metal cations (Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺, Fe²⁺) through its β-diketone moiety. Fluoroquinolones form chelation complexes with polyvalent cations in the GI tract, reducing absorption — curcumin's metal chelation may compete with or displace these drug-metal complexes, unpredictably affecting fluoroquinolone absorption. Also potential for synergistic antibacterial activity (independently beneficial).
effect: Slightly variable fluoroquinolone absorption. Clinical significance is generally low but potentially relevant in serious infections requiring precise drug levels.
management: Separate curcumin and fluoroquinolone dosing by at least 2 hours during antibiotic therapy. Take fluoroquinolone on empty stomach. Generally minor concern at standard doses.
references: Atal N et al. Phytomedicine 2012;19:1174–1180

mechanism: High-dose garlic supplements may have mild CYP3A4 modulating activity. Etonogestrel is primarily metabolised by CYP3A4. The interaction is theoretically present but pharmacokinetically not yet well-characterised for long-acting formulations.
effect: Potentially reduced progestin levels with very high-dose garlic supplementation — clinical significance for contraceptive failure risk is currently considered very low.
management: No action required at standard garlic supplementation doses. Advise patients on etonogestrel implant to use standard-dose garlic supplements only and avoid megadosing. Current evidence does not support switching contraception method.
references: Sparreboom A et al. J Natl Cancer Inst 2004;96:1365–1369

mechanism: Rifampicin is a potent inducer of CYP3A4, CYP2C9, and P-gp. Black cohosh triterpene glycosides (actein, 27-deoxyactein) are metabolised by CYP3A4. Rifampicin may significantly reduce active black cohosh constituent exposure, reducing its clinical efficacy (e.g., for menopausal symptom management). Conversely, reduced triterpene levels may reduce hepatotoxicity risk.
effect: Reduced efficacy of black cohosh for menopausal symptoms, return of vasomotor symptoms (hot flushes, night sweats). Interaction likely to be clinically meaningful given rifampicin's potent enzyme induction.
management: Advise patients on rifampicin-based TB or MAC therapy that black cohosh may be ineffective. Consider alternative menopausal symptom management (HRT under specialist guidance if appropriate).
references: Gurley BJ et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2005;77:415–426

mechanism: Ashwagandha stimulates thyroid gland activity (increases TSH response and thyroid peroxidase activity) and has been shown in a human RCT to elevate both T3 and T4 in subclinical hypothyroid patients. When combined with exogenous thyroid hormone replacement, cumulative thyroid hormone bioavailability increases above the therapeutic range.
effect: Iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis: palpitations, atrial fibrillation, weight loss, heat intolerance, anxiety, osteoporosis risk with prolonged exposure.
management: Monitor TSH and free T4 closely (at 6–8 weeks and then 6-monthly) when ashwagandha is added to thyroid hormone therapy. May require dose reduction of levothyroxine by 12.5–25 mcg. Beneficial effect in subclinical hypothyroidism but requires careful titration.
references: Sharma AK et al. J Altern Complement Med 2018;24:243–248

mechanism: Ginkgolide B antagonises platelet-activating factor (PAF) receptor with high affinity, producing independent antiplatelet activity alongside anticoagulation. Combined with anticoagulants of any class (heparins, DOACs), the dual impairment of haemostasis (primary + secondary) significantly increases haemorrhagic risk. Ginkgo also has weak thromboxane synthesis inhibitory activity.
effect: Significantly elevated bleeding risk across all anticoagulated patients — including those on DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran) which do not have reversal agents readily available. Intracranial haemorrhage, GI bleeding, surgical haemorrhage.
management: Avoid ginkgo in all anticoagulated patients regardless of drug class. Mandatory cessation at least 7 days prior to any invasive procedure. Screen all patients on DOACs — this interaction is frequently overlooked.
references: Jiang X et al. J Clin Pharmacol 2005;45:1484–1491
Braun L et al. Herb-Drug Interactions: A clinical review 2010

mechanism: Valerenic acid's GABAergic potentiation produces additive CNS depression with opioids that depress the respiratory centre via mu-opioid receptors in the brainstem. GABA and opioid signalling interact in the respiratory centres — combined suppression produces supra-additive respiratory depression risk.
effect: Excessive sedation, respiratory depression, apnoea especially during sleep (particularly relevant in obstructive sleep apnoea patients). Risk highest at night when patients may use valerian for insomnia and opioids for pain management concurrently.
management: Avoid combination, particularly in patients with OSA, COPD, or elderly patients. If both prescribed, titrate opioid dose conservatively. Consider alternative sleep management (sleep hygiene, CBT-I) in patients on opioids.
references: Huang YS et al. J Pharm Sci 2003;92:2181–2189

mechanism: Piperine (bioperine) from black pepper is commonly added to curcumin supplements to enhance bioavailability by 2000%. Piperine itself inhibits UDP-glucuronosyltransferases (UGTs) and P-glycoprotein, and modulates CYP enzymes. This significantly alters the absorption and metabolism of multiple drugs. The curcumin-piperine combination thus has substantially greater drug interaction potential than curcumin alone.
effect: Variable elevation of co-administered drugs whose metabolism is inhibited by piperine: phenytoin toxicity (ataxia, nystagmus), propranolol (bradycardia, hypotension), theophylline toxicity (arrhythmias, seizures). The interaction profile varies widely by drug.
management: Always ask patients whether their curcumin supplement contains piperine/BioPerine. If so, treat the interaction risk as substantially higher than for curcumin alone. Avoid curcumin-piperine combination in patients on narrow-TI drugs. Plain curcumin or phosphatidylcholine-enhanced formulations are safer alternatives.
references: Shoba G et al. Planta Med 1998;64:353–356
Atal N et al. Phytomedicine 2012;19:1174–1180
